title: Hogan: Council, not appointed board, should have final say on historic properties (Your Letters)
summary: Common Councilor Patrick Hogan defends proposed legislation to shift final decision-making authority on already-designated historic properties from the appointed Landmark Preservation Board to the elected Common Council. Hogan argues this ensures democratic accountability and balances historic preservation with present-day neighborhood and economic development needs, while maintaining the Council's existing role in the designation process itself.

political_signal.description: Hogan actively advocates for legislative change to reallocate authority from appointed preservation board to elected council, framing it as democratic accountability and balanced governance.

processing_notes: Letter is a direct response to prior coverage; author is responding to criticism of proposed legislation
No budget impacts or dollar amounts mentioned
This represents governance/process innovation with implications for land-use and economic development
Potential stakeholder tension between preservation advocates and development/housing interests
